summary: This notebook implements a production-ready inference pipeline for the NFL 2026 player tracking competition. It initializes a Kaggle evaluation server, loads pre-trained `STTransformer` models and scalers, and processes test data into sequences with advanced features. Predictions are generated using test-time augmentation across an ensemble of models, then transformed back to original field coordinates based on play direction before submission. Readers should learn how to structure efficient, cached inference loops, handle direction-dependent coordinate systems, and leverage TTA for robust competition submissions.
competition_flow: Loads pre-trained `STTransformer` models and scalers, processes test data into sequences with advanced features, generates predictions via an ensemble with test-time augmentation, applies coordinate transformations, and submits results through Kaggle's evaluation API.
data_reading: Reads test batches as `polars.DataFrame`, converts them to `pandas` for processing, and uses `kaggle_evaluation.nfl_inference_server` to fetch data in the competition environment.
data_processing: Converts Polars to Pandas, constructs sequences with advanced features, applies test-time augmentation during prediction, clips coordinates to field boundaries, and inverts coordinates based on `play_direction`.
features_engineering: Advanced features generated by `prepare_sequences_with_advanced_features` (exact transformations not visible in this cell).
ensembling: Averages predictions across an ensemble of `STTransformer` models, each processed with test-time augmentation (TTA).
insights: Pre-loading models once and caching them avoids repeated initialization overhead during inference.
Test-time augmentation (TTA) across multiple model checkpoints improves prediction stability.
Coordinate inversion based on play_direction is necessary to align predictions with the original field orientation.
